title: Cloud-Based Productivity Tools
obj: Students will be able to identify major cloud productivity suites, describe how they differ from desktop software, and configure basic collaboration features.
content: Cloud vs. desktop apps
always updated, accessible anywhere, subscription pricing, internet dependency
Microsoft 365
Word/Excel/PowerPoint online, OneDrive, Teams, SharePoint; licensing tiers
Google Workspace
Docs/Sheets/Slides, Drive, Gmail, Meet, Classroom; free vs. paid tiers
Collaboration features
real-time co-editing, comments, version history, sharing permissions
Sync and offline access
desktop sync clients, offline mode in browser, conflict resolution
IT support considerations
licensing management, SSO integration, data retention policies, BYOD access
assess: Side-by-side task: complete the same formatting task in Word desktop vs. Word Online; note differences
Sharing lab: share a Google Doc with specific permissions and verify a classmate's access level
Short answer: what is one business risk of storing company documents in a personal cloud account?

title: User Accounts, Permissions, and ACLs
obj: Students will be able to create and manage user accounts, assign NTFS and share permissions, and explain how ACLs control access to resources.
content: User account types
local vs. domain accounts; Administrator vs. Standard; built-in accounts (Guest, DefaultAccount)
Windows user management
Local Users and Groups MMC; net user command; creating, disabling, and deleting accounts
NTFS permissions
Read, Write, Modify, Full Control, List; explicit vs. inherited; how deny overrides allow
Share permissions
Full Control, Change, Read; how share and NTFS permissions combine (most restrictive wins)
ACL structure
DACL vs. SACL; ACEs (access control entries); viewing with icacls command
Principle of least privilege
give users only the access they need; auditing access; removing stale accounts
assess: Lab: create a folder, set NTFS permissions for three different users, and verify each user's access
icacls exercise: use icacls to view and modify permissions on a test folder from the command line
Short answer: a user has Read NTFS permission but Full Control share permission — what can they actually do?

title: Filesystems and Disk Partitioning
obj: Students will be able to create and manage disk partitions, format volumes with the appropriate file system, and use Disk Management and CLI tools to perform disk operations.
content: MBR vs. GPT
MBR limits (2 TB, 4 primary partitions); GPT advantages (128 partitions, UEFI); when each is used
Partition types
primary, extended, logical on MBR; EFI System Partition; recovery partitions
Windows Disk Management
initialize a disk, create/delete/format/extend volumes; assign drive letters; convert MBR↔GPT
diskpart CLI
list disk/partition/volume, select, clean, create partition primary, format, assign; scripting disk setup
Linux partitioning
fdisk, gdisk, parted; /dev/sda naming; mounting partitions; /etc/fstab for persistence
Volume types
simple, spanned, striped (RAID-0), mirrored (RAID-1) in Windows; LVM basics on Linux
assess: Lab: attach a virtual disk to a VM, initialize as GPT, create two partitions, format each, and assign drive letters
diskpart script: write a diskpart script that would prepare a blank USB drive as a bootable FAT32 volume
Short answer: why can't a 3 TB drive be fully used with an MBR partition scheme?

title: Process Management and System Resources
obj: Students will be able to view and manage running processes, identify resource-hungry tasks, and take corrective action when a system is unresponsive.
content: Processes vs. threads
a process is an isolated program instance; threads share a process's memory; context switching
Windows tools
Task Manager (Processes/Details tabs), Resource Monitor, tasklist/taskkill commands
Linux tools
ps aux, top, htop, kill/killall, nice/renice; process states (R/S/Z)
Memory management
RAM usage, virtual memory/page file, commit charge; what happens when RAM is exhausted
CPU and disk bottlenecks
identifying high CPU vs. high I/O wait; which resource is the actual bottleneck
Startup and scheduled tasks
msconfig startup, Task Scheduler, cron jobs; disabling unnecessary startup items
assess: Lab: use Task Manager and Resource Monitor to identify the process causing high CPU during a simulated load
Kill task: find and terminate a hung process using both GUI and CLI methods
Short answer: what is a zombie process and why does it indicate a programming bug?

title: Remote Access and Virtual Machines
obj: Students will be able to configure and use remote desktop tools, and create and manage a basic virtual machine using a hosted hypervisor.
content: Remote Desktop Protocol
enabling RDP on Windows; client connection; firewall rules; port 3389; NLA requirement
SSH remote access
key-based vs. password auth; PuTTY and Windows Terminal; basic SSH commands for remote admin
VNC and third-party tools
TeamViewer, AnyDesk, Chrome Remote Desktop; use cases vs. RDP/SSH; attended vs. unattended
Hosted hypervisors
VMware Workstation, VirtualBox, Hyper-V (Windows Pro); guest additions/tools; snapshot workflow
VM configuration
vCPU count, RAM allocation, disk type (dynamic vs. fixed), network modes (NAT/bridged/host-only)
Use cases for VMs
safe testing environment, running legacy software, dev/test isolation, sandboxing
assess: RDP lab: enable RDP on a Windows VM and connect to it from the host machine
VM creation: create a new VM in VirtualBox, install an OS, and take a snapshot before making changes
Short answer: what is the difference between NAT and bridged networking mode in a VM?

title: Productivity, Collaboration, and Business Software
obj: Students will be able to identify major categories of business software, describe common applications in each category, and match software to a business use case.
content: Productivity suites
word processing, spreadsheets, presentations, email clients — Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace
Communication tools
email (Outlook, Gmail), chat (Teams, Slack), video conferencing (Zoom, Meet), VoIP
Collaboration and project management
SharePoint, Confluence, Asana, Jira, Trello — purpose and typical users
Industry-specific software
ERP (SAP), CRM (Salesforce), accounting (QuickBooks), HRIS, healthcare EMR
Creative and multimedia
Adobe Creative Suite, video editors, CAD tools — heavy hardware requirements
Security and management software
antivirus, backup software, patch management, MDM, RMM tools used by IT staff
assess: Software sort: given a list of 15 applications, categorize each by type
Scenario: a small business needs email, file storage, and video meetings — recommend a suite and justify
Short answer: what is the difference between CRM and ERP software?

title: Application Architecture and Cloud Delivery Models
obj: Students will be able to distinguish client-server, web, and cloud application architectures, and compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS delivery models.
content: Client-server architecture
client sends requests; server processes and responds; two-tier vs. three-tier; middleware
Web applications
browser as client; HTTP/HTTPS; stateless vs. stateful; front-end vs. back-end; APIs
SaaS
software fully hosted and managed by vendor; user accesses via browser; examples: Salesforce, Gmail
PaaS
provider manages OS and runtime; developer deploys code; examples: Heroku, Azure App Service
IaaS
provider supplies virtual hardware; customer manages OS up; examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs
Hybrid and multi-cloud
mixing cloud providers and on-premises; why organizations avoid single-vendor lock-in
assess: Model classification: given 8 services, identify each as SaaS/PaaS/IaaS
Architecture diagram: draw a three-tier web app showing browser, web server, app server, and database
Short answer: why would a startup choose SaaS email over running their own mail server?

title: Web Browser Configuration and Safety
obj: Students will be able to configure browser privacy and security settings, manage extensions, and identify common browser-based threats.
content: Major browsers
Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari — market share, rendering engines, sync features
Privacy settings
cookies (first-party vs. third-party), do not track, private/incognito mode limitations
Security settings
HTTPS-only mode, certificate warnings, safe browsing, pop-up blocker, download scanning
Extensions and plugins
legitimate productivity extensions vs. malicious browser hijackers; reviewing permissions
Browser data management
clearing cache, history, cookies, saved passwords; impact on performance and privacy
Enterprise browser management
group policy to lock settings, managed extensions, homepage enforcement
assess: Configuration lab: lock down a browser per a provided security checklist (block pop-ups, HTTPS-only, disable autofill)
Extension audit: review the permissions requested by three extensions and classify each as safe/risky
Short answer: why does clearing the cache sometimes fix a broken website?
